summary: Three‑set marathon with The Other One > Eyes jam and killer Wolf guitar
blurb: The show shines with a soaring The Other One into Eyes of the World, followed by a fiery China Doll, while Jerry’s new Wolf guitar roars on Playing In The Band. Phil’s bass is explosive and Keith’s piano adds a Tyner‑like lift. A must‑listen for any 1973 Dead fan.
review: On May 13 1973 the Grateful Dead turned the Des Moines State Fairgrounds into a rain‑to‑sunshine spectacle, complete with a double rainbow and three marathon sets that still feel fresh today. Set 1 rolls through classic Americana before handing the reins to a second set highlighted by Here Comes Sunshine and an incandescent Playing In The Band where Jerry’s freshly‑minted Wolf guitar shreds like never before. The real fireworks erupt in Set 3: after a tight He’s Gone → Truckin! → Drums bridge, The Other One blossoms into a sprawling jam that seamlessly morphs into Eyes Of The World and lands on a beautifully rendered China Doll, showcasing Keith’s McCoy Tyner‑style piano voicings and Phil’s bass that some listeners describe as “cello‑like” in its depth. With the band locked in, the performance is a quintessential 1973 snapshot—tight, exploratory, and endlessly rewarding.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All five recordings are positive or mixed, with multiple sources praising the same moments: The Other One > Eyes of the World sequence, Jerry’s Wolf guitar tone, and Phil’s explosive bass. No consistent negative comments about playing quality appear, yielding a strong positive sentiment adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Good soundboard capture that, despite some hiss, presents a clear mix with Jerry’s guitar front‑and‑center and preserves the marathon three‑set flow.

key_highlights: Seamless The Other One → Eyes Of The World → China Doll sequence in Set 3
Jerry’s new Wolf guitar tone shines on Playing In The Band and throughout the jams
Phil’s bass delivers explosive, cello‑like depth especially during the long second‑set jams
Keith’s piano on Eyes Of The World adds a McCoy Tyner‑style harmonic lift

band_performance.Jerry: Prominent mix with his Wolf guitar delivering stellar solos, especially on The Other One and Playing In The Band.
band_performance.Phil: Explosive, high‑energy bass work described as ‘cello‑like’ and dropping bombs throughout the set.
band_performance.Keith: Piano on Eyes Of The World noted for its McCoy Tyner‑like sound; overall keyboard work well regarded.
